revision.text:
'''倭 手彦'''（やまと の てひこ、生没年不詳）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]。[[倭国造]]の一人。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直 (姓)|直]]。
== 出自 == 倭氏（[[倭国造]]家）は[[大和国]][[城下郡]]大和郷（おおやまとごう）の豪族で、この地域は現在の[[奈良県]][[天理市]]の南部、佐保町大和にあたる。『[[新撰姓氏録]]』「大和国神別」では「出自神知津彦命也」と記されており、『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三では[[椎根津彦]]（しいねつひこ）<ref>『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条</ref>、『[[古事記]]』では槁根津日子（さおねつひこ）を祖とする<ref>『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条</ref>、となっている。
== 記録 == 西暦[[562年]]（[[欽明天皇]]23年）、任那の官家が新羅に滅ぼされてから<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref>、欽明天皇は[[任那日本府]]の再興を悲願とするようになった。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九には、[[新羅]]征討軍の大将軍、[[紀男麻呂]]（き の おまろ）と[[河辺瓊缶]]（かわべ の にえ）の物語がある。男麻呂は新羅が偽装降伏してきた時点で誘いには乗らずに進軍を止めたが、瓊缶はさらに奮戦し、新羅側が白旗をあげた意味を理解せずに、同じように白旗をあげて進んでいった。そのため、新羅側の再攻撃を受け、先鋒の軍に大損害を与えた。
瓊缶の部下として参戦していた倭国造手彦は、もはや戦況を挽回することは不可能だと判断し、戦線から離脱した。新羅の闘将（いくさのきみ）は手に矛をとって追いかけ、彼を城の溝に追い詰め、矛を投げたが、手彦は駿馬を操り、溝を乗り越えて辛うじて身を脱した。闘将はくやしがって、思わず声に出して叫んでいた。
この戦いで、新羅の闘将は瓊缶や同行していた婦女たちを捕虜にした。瓊缶は妻の[[甘美媛]]（うましひめ）を身代わりにして、解放され、生きながらえた。妻は闘将の妾にされ、その後解放されたが、瓊缶の言うことに従おうとはしなかった。
倭手彦がその後どうなったのかは、記録されていないため、不明である<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref>。
『書紀』巻第二十九によると、[[681年]][[天武天皇]]10年4月）、倭直竜麻呂は[[連]]の[[カバネ|姓]]を与えられ<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条</ref>、12年9月には倭氏一族すべてに連姓が授けられた<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条</ref>。そして、[[八色の姓]]制定により、[[国造]]系[[氏族]]である大倭氏（やまとうじ）は[[忌寸]]を賜姓されている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（一）・(三)・（五）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年、1995年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）・(下）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』、[[井上光貞]]：著、[[中央公論社]]、1965年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編、[[雄山閣]]、2015年
== 関連項目 == *[[調伊企儺]] *[[大葉子]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:やまと の てひこ}} [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:倭氏|てひこ]] [[Category:古代日朝関係]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

revision.text:
'''倭 手彦'''（やまと の てひこ、生没年不詳）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]。[[倭国造]]の一人。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直 (姓)|直]]。
== 出自 == 倭氏（[[倭国造]]家）は[[大和国]][[城下郡]]大和郷（おおやまとごう）の豪族で、この地域は現在の[[奈良県]][[天理市]]の南部、佐保町大和にあたる。『[[新撰姓氏録]]』「大和国神別」では「出自神知津彦命也」と記されており、『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三では[[椎根津彦]]（しいねつひこ）<ref>『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条</ref>、『[[古事記]]』では槁根津日子（さおねつひこ）を祖とする<ref>『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条</ref>、となっている。
== 記録 == 西暦[[562年]]（[[欽明天皇]]23年）、任那の官家が新羅に滅ぼされてから<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref>、欽明天皇は[[任那日本府]]の再興を悲願とするようになった。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九には、[[新羅]]征討軍の大将軍、[[紀男麻呂]]（き の おまろ）と[[河辺瓊缶]]（かわべ の にえ）の物語がある。男麻呂は新羅が偽装降伏してきた時点で誘いには乗らずに進軍を止めたが、瓊缶はさらに奮戦し、新羅側が白旗をあげた意味を理解せずに、同じように白旗をあげて進んでいった。そのため、新羅側の再攻撃を受け、先鋒の軍に大損害を与えた。
瓊缶の部下として参戦していた倭国造手彦は、もはや戦況を挽回することは不可能だと判断し、戦線から離脱した。新羅の闘将（いくさのきみ）は手に矛をとって追いかけ、彼を城の溝に追い詰め、矛を投げたが、手彦は駿馬を操り、溝を乗り越えて辛うじて身を脱した。闘将はくやしがって、思わず声に出して叫んでいた。
この戦いで、新羅の闘将は瓊缶や同行していた婦女たちを捕虜にした。瓊缶は妻の[[甘美媛]]（うましひめ）を身代わりにして、解放され、生きながらえた。妻は闘将の妾にされ、その後解放されたが、瓊缶の言うことに従おうとはしなかった。
倭手彦がその後どうなったのかは、記録されていないため、不明である<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref>。
『書紀』巻第二十九によると、[[681年]]（[[天武天皇]]10年4月）、一族の倭直竜麻呂は「[[連]]」の[[カバネ|姓]]を与えられ<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条</ref>、天武天皇12年9月には、倭氏一族すべてに「連」姓が授けられた<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条</ref>。そして、[[八色の姓]]制定により、[[685年]]（天武天皇14年6月）、[[国造]]系[[氏族]]である大倭氏（やまとうじ）は[[忌寸]]を賜姓されている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（一）・(三)・（五）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年、1995年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）・(下）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』、[[井上光貞]]：著、[[中央公論社]]、1965年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編、[[雄山閣]]、2015年
== 関連項目 == *[[調伊企儺]] *[[大葉子]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:やまと の てひこ}} [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:倭氏|てひこ]] [[Category:古代日朝関係]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九には、[[新羅]]征討軍の大将軍、[[紀男麻呂]]（き の おまろ）と[[河辺瓊缶]]（かわべ の にえ）の物語がある。男麻呂は新羅が偽装降伏してきた時点で誘いには乗らずに進軍を止めたが、瓊缶はさらに奮戦し、新羅側が白旗をあげた意味を理解せずに、同じように白旗をあげて進んでいった。そのため、新羅側の再攻撃を受け、先鋒の軍に大損害を与えた。

この戦いで、新羅の闘将は瓊缶や同行していた婦女たちを捕虜にした。瓊缶は妻の[[甘美媛]]（うましひめ）を身代わりにして、解放され、生きながらえた。妻は闘将の妾にされ、その後解放されたが、瓊缶の言うことに従おうとはしなかった。
倭手彦がその後どうなったのかは、記録されていないため、不明である<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref>。
『書紀』巻第二十九によると、[[681年]]（[[天武天皇]]10年4月）、一族の倭直竜麻呂は「[[連]]」の[[カバネ|姓]]を与えられ<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条</ref>、天武天皇12年9月には、倭氏一族すべてに「連」姓が授けられた<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条</ref>。そして、[[八色の姓]]制定により、[[685年]]（天武天皇14年6月）、[[国造]]系[[氏族]]である大倭氏（やまとうじ）は[[忌寸]]を賜姓されている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（一）・(三)・（五）、[[岩波文庫]]、1994年、1995年 * 『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）・(下）、[[講談社学術文庫]]、[[宇治谷孟]]：訳、1988年 * 『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』、[[井上光貞]]：著、[[中央公論社]]、1965年 * 『日本古代氏族事典』【新装版】[[佐伯有清]]：編、[[雄山閣]]、2015年

『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九には、[[新羅]]征討軍の大将軍、[[紀男麻呂]]（き の おまろ）と[[河辺瓊缶]]（かわべ の にえ）の物語がある。男麻呂は新羅が偽装降伏してきた時点で誘いには乗らずに進軍を止めたが、瓊缶はさらに奮戦し、新羅側が白旗をあげた意味を理解せずに、同じように白旗をあげて進んでいった。そのため、新羅側の再攻撃を受け、先鋒の軍に大損害を与えた。
瓊缶の部下として参戦していた倭国造手彦は、もはや戦況を挽回することは不可能だと判断し、戦線から離脱した。新羅の闘将（いくさのきみ）は手に矛をとって追いかけ、彼を城の溝に追い詰め、矛を投げたが、手彦は駿馬を操り、溝を乗り越えて辛うじて身を脱した。闘将はくやしがって、思わず声に出して叫んでいた。
この戦いで、新羅の闘将は瓊缶や同行していた婦女たちを捕虜にした。瓊缶は妻の[[甘美媛]]（うましひめ）を身代わりにして、解放され、生きながらえた。妻は闘将の妾にされ、その後解放されたが、瓊缶の言うことに従おうとはしなかった。
倭手彦がその後どうなったのかは、記録されていないため、不明である<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref>。
『書紀』巻第二十九によると、[[681年]]（[[天武天皇]]10年4月）、一族の倭直竜麻呂は「[[連]]」の[[カバネ|姓]]を与えられ<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条</ref>、天武天皇12年9月には、倭氏一族すべてに「連」姓が授けられた<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条</ref>。そして、[[八色の姓]]制定により、[[685年]]（天武天皇14年6月）、[[国造]]系[[氏族]]である大倭氏（やまとうじ）は[[忌寸]]を賜姓されている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条</ref>。

{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 倭手彦 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = [[倭国造]] | 主君 = [[欽明天皇]] | 氏族 = [[倭氏|倭直]] | 父母 = 父：[[由岐庭乃直]] | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = [[倭邑智|邑智]] | 特記事項 = }} '''倭 手彦'''（やまと の てひこ、[[生没年不詳]]）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]。[[倭国造]]の一人。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直 (姓)|直]]。
== 出自 == 倭氏（[[倭国造]]家）は[[大和国]][[城下郡]]大和郷（おおやまとごう）の豪族で、この地域は現在の[[奈良県]][[天理市]]の南部、佐保町大和にあたる。『[[新撰姓氏録]]』「大和国神別」では「出自神知津彦命也」と記されており、『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三では[[椎根津彦]]（しいねつひこ）<ref>『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条</ref>、『[[古事記]]』では槁根津日子（さおねつひこ）を祖とする<ref>『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条</ref>、となっている。
== 記録 == 西暦[[562年]]（[[欽明天皇]]23年）、任那の官家が新羅に滅ぼされてから<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref>、欽明天皇は[[任那日本府]]の再興を悲願とするようになった。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九には、[[新羅]]征討軍の大将軍、[[紀男麻呂]]（き の おまろ）と[[河辺瓊缶]]（かわべ の にえ）の物語がある。男麻呂は新羅が偽装降伏してきた時点で誘いには乗らずに進軍を止めたが、瓊缶はさらに奮戦し、新羅側が白旗をあげた意味を理解せずに、同じように白旗をあげて進んでいった。そのため、新羅側の再攻撃を受け、先鋒の軍に大損害を与えた。
瓊缶の部下として参戦していた倭国造手彦は、もはや戦況を挽回することは不可能だと判断し、戦線から離脱した。新羅の闘将（いくさのきみ）は手に矛をとって追いかけ、彼を城の溝に追い詰め、矛を投げたが、手彦は駿馬を操り、溝を乗り越えて辛うじて身を脱した。闘将はくやしがって、思わず声に出して叫んでいた。
この戦いで、新羅の闘将は瓊缶や同行していた婦女たちを捕虜にした。瓊缶は妻の[[甘美媛]]（うましひめ）を身代わりにして、解放され、生きながらえた。妻は闘将の妾にされ、その後解放されたが、瓊缶の言うことに従おうとはしなかった。
倭手彦がその後どうなったのかは、記録されていないため、不明である<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref>。
『書紀』巻第二十九によると、[[681年]]（[[天武天皇]]10年4月）、一族の倭直竜麻呂は「[[連]]」の[[カバネ|姓]]を与えられ<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条</ref>、天武天皇12年9月には、倭氏一族すべてに「連」姓が授けられた<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条</ref>。そして、[[八色の姓]]制定により、[[685年]]（天武天皇14年6月）、[[国造]]系[[氏族]]である大倭氏（やまとうじ）は[[忌寸]]を賜姓されている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条</ref>。

『書紀』巻第二十九によると、[[681年]]（[[天武天皇]]10年4月）、一族の倭直竜麻呂は「[[連]]」の[[カバネ|姓]]を与えられ<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条</ref>、天武天皇12年9月には、倭氏一族すべてに「連」姓が授けられた<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条</ref>。そして、[[八色の姓]]制定により、[[685年]]（天武天皇14年6月）、[[国造]]系[[氏族]]である大倭氏（やまとうじ）は[[忌寸]]を賜姓されている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条</ref>。 == 系譜 == {{ワタツミの子孫}}
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（一）・(三)・（五）（[[岩波文庫]]、1994年、1995年） *[[宇治谷孟]]訳 『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）・(下）（[[講談社学術文庫]]、1988年） *[[井上光貞]]『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』（[[中央公論社]]、1965年） *[[佐伯有清]]編『日本古代氏族事典【新装版】』（[[雄山閣]]、2015年）
== 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] *[[調伊企儺]] *[[大葉子]]

revision.comment: 戻し：典拠不明であり掲載不適当と判断。
revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 倭手彦 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = [[倭国造]] | 主君 = [[欽明天皇]] | 氏族 = [[倭氏|倭直]] | 父母 = | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = }} '''倭 手彦'''（やまと の てひこ、[[生没年不詳]]）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]で'''[[倭国造]]'''の一人。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直 (姓)|直]]。
== 出自 == 倭氏（[[倭国造]]家）は[[大和国]][[城下郡]]大和郷（おおやまとごう）の豪族で、この地域は現在の[[奈良県]][[天理市]]の南部、佐保町大和にあたる。『[[新撰姓氏録]]』「大和国神別」では「出自神知津彦命也」と記されており、『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三では[[椎根津彦]]（しいねつひこ）<ref>『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条</ref>、『[[古事記]]』では槁根津日子（さおねつひこ）を祖とする<ref>『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条</ref>、となっている。
== 記録 == 西暦[[562年]]（[[欽明天皇]]23年）、任那の官家が新羅に滅ぼされてから<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref>、欽明天皇は[[任那日本府]]の再興を悲願とするようになった。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九には、[[新羅]]征討軍の大将軍、[[紀男麻呂]]（き の おまろ）と[[河辺瓊缶]]（かわべ の にえ）の物語がある。男麻呂は新羅が偽装降伏してきた時点で誘いには乗らずに進軍を止めたが、瓊缶はさらに奮戦し、新羅側が白旗をあげた意味を理解せずに、同じように白旗をあげて進んでいった。そのため、新羅側の再攻撃を受け、先鋒の軍に大損害を与えた。
瓊缶の部下として参戦していた倭国造手彦は、もはや戦況を挽回することは不可能だと判断し、戦線から離脱した。新羅の闘将（いくさのきみ）は手に矛をとって追いかけ、彼を城の溝に追い詰め、矛を投げたが、手彦は駿馬を操り、溝を乗り越えて辛うじて身を脱した。闘将はくやしがって、思わず声に出して叫んでいた。
この戦いで、新羅の闘将は瓊缶や同行していた婦女たちを捕虜にした。瓊缶は妻の[[甘美媛]]（うましひめ）を身代わりにして、解放され、生きながらえた。妻は闘将の妾にされ、その後解放されたが、瓊缶の言うことに従おうとはしなかった。
倭手彦がその後どうなったのかは、記録されていないため、不明である<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref>。
『書紀』巻第二十九によると、[[681年]]（[[天武天皇]]10年4月）、一族の倭直竜麻呂は「[[連]]」の[[カバネ|姓]]を与えられ<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条</ref>、天武天皇12年9月には、倭氏一族すべてに「連」姓が授けられた<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条</ref>。そして、[[八色の姓]]制定により、[[685年]]（天武天皇14年6月）、[[国造]]系[[氏族]]である大倭氏（やまとうじ）は[[忌寸]]を賜姓されている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（一）・(三)・（五）（[[岩波文庫]]、1994年、1995年） *[[宇治谷孟]]訳 『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）・(下）（[[講談社学術文庫]]、1988年） *[[井上光貞]]『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』（[[中央公論社]]、1965年） *[[佐伯有清]]編『日本古代氏族事典【新装版】』（[[雄山閣]]、2015年）
== 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] *[[調伊企儺]] *[[大葉子]]

revision.comment: 戻し：「国造主」という学術用語なし。勝手な造語につき。

revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/99874815|倭手彦]]"
revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家|氏名=倭手彦|画像=|画像サイズ=|画像説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=}}'''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and [[:ja:生没年不詳|death unknown]] ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Wa kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was [[直 (姓)|Atai]] == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=ISSE|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986|jstor=26864868}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web|title=海部氏系図|trans-title=Amebe shikeizu|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777|publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]|language=Japanese|accessdate=August 20, 2020}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} == record == In 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei's reign]] ), after the Mimana government was destroyed by Silla 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei fervently wished to restore [[:ja:任那日本府|the Mimana Nihonfu]] . According to Volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, [[681|in 681]] (April 10th year of [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu's reign]] ), a member of the clan, Yamato Naotatsumaro, was given the surname " [[Muraji]] " 『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条, and in September 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, the surname "Muraji" was bestowed on all members of the Yamato clan. 『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条 With the establishment of the eight-rank system of surnames, in 685 (June 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Yamato]] clan, a clan descended from [[Clan|the kuni]] no miyatsuko, was given the surname Ikimi . 『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 Volume 19 of the " [[Nihon Shoki]] " contains the story of [[:ja:紀男麻呂|Kino Omaro]], the commander-in-chief of the army that conquered [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. When Silla made a fake surrender, Otokomaro did not accept the invitation and stopped his advance, but Kyokan fought even harder and, not understanding the meaning of the white flag raised by the Silla side, also raised the white flag and advanced. As a result, the Silla side attacked again, causing heavy losses to the vanguard forces. Wa no Kuni no Miyatsuko Tehiko, who had been fighting under Nenkan, decided that it was no longer possible to turn the tide of battle and withdrew from the front lines. The Silla warrior took up a spear and pursued him, cornering him in a ditch in the castle and throwing his spear at him, but Tehiko rode his swift horse over the ditch and barely escaped. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * [[:ja:飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[:ja:調伊企儺|調伊企儺]] * [[:ja:大葉子|大葉子]]

{{基礎情報 公家|氏名=倭手彦|画像=|画像サイズ=|画像説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=}}'''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and [[:ja:生没年不詳|death unknown]] ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Wa kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was [[直 (姓)|Atai]] == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=ISSE|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986|jstor=26864868}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web|title=海部氏系図|trans-title=Amebe shikeizu|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777|publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]|language=Japanese|accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[ja:倭手彦]][[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * [[:ja:飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[:ja:調伊企儺|調伊企儺]] * [[:ja:大葉子|大葉子]]

{{基礎情報 公家|氏名=倭手彦|画像=|画像サイズ=|画像説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=}}'''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and [[:ja:生没年不詳|death unknown]] ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Wa kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was [[直 (姓)|Atai]] == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=ISSE|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986|jstor=26864868}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web|title=海部氏系図|trans-title=Amebe shikeizu|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777|publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]|language=Japanese|accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[ja:倭手彦]][[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} == record == In 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei's reign]] ), after the Mimana government was destroyed by Silla 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei fervently wished to restore [[:ja:任那日本府|the Mimana Nihonfu]] . According to Volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, [[681|in 681]] (April 10th year of [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu's reign]] ), a member of the clan, Yamato Naotatsumaro, was given the surname " [[Muraji]] " 『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条, and in September 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, the surname "Muraji" was bestowed on all members of the Yamato clan. 『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条 With the establishment of the eight-rank system of surnames, in 685 (June 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Yamato]] clan, a clan descended from [[Clan|the kuni]] no miyatsuko, was given the surname Ikimi . 『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 Volume 19 of the " [[Nihon Shoki]] " contains the story of [[:ja:紀男麻呂|Kino Omaro]], the commander-in-chief of the army that conquered [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. When Silla made a fake surrender, Otokomaro did not accept the invitation and stopped his advance, but Kyokan fought even harder and, not understanding the meaning of the white flag raised by the Silla side, also raised the white flag and advanced. As a result, the Silla side attacked again, causing heavy losses to the vanguard forces. Wa no Kuni no Miyatsuko Tehiko, who had been fighting under Nenkan, decided that it was no longer possible to turn the tide of battle and withdrew from the front lines. The Silla warrior took up a spear and pursued him, cornering him in a ditch in the castle and throwing his spear at him, but Tehiko rode his swift horse over the ditch and barely escaped. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * [[:ja:飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[:ja:調伊企儺|調伊企儺]] * [[:ja:大葉子|大葉子]] [[Category:Draft articles]]{{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]][[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}[[Category:draft articles]]

Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} == record == In 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei's reign]] ), after the Mimana government was destroyed by Silla 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei fervently wished to restore [[:ja:任那日本府|the Mimana Nihonfu]] . According to Volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, [[681|in 681]] (April 10th year of [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu's reign]] ), a member of the clan, Yamato Naotatsumaro, was given the surname " [[Muraji]] " 『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条, and in September 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, the surname "Muraji" was bestowed on all members of the Yamato clan. 『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条 With the establishment of the eight-rank system of surnames, in 685 (June 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Yamato]] clan, a clan descended from [[Clan|the kuni]] no miyatsuko, was given the surname Ikimi . 『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 Volume 19 of the " [[Nihon Shoki]] " contains the story of [[:ja:紀男麻呂|Kino Omaro]], the commander-in-chief of the army that conquered [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. When Silla made a fake surrender, Otokomaro did not accept the invitation and stopped his advance, but Kyokan fought even harder and, not understanding the meaning of the white flag raised by the Silla side, also raised the white flag and advanced. As a result, the Silla side attacked again, causing heavy losses to the vanguard forces. Wa no Kuni no Miyatsuko Tehiko, who had been fighting under Nenkan, decided that it was no longer possible to turn the tide of battle and withdrew from the front lines. The Silla warrior took up a spear and pursued him, cornering him in a ditch in the castle and throwing his spear at him, but Tehiko rode his swift horse over the ditch and barely escaped. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * [[:ja:飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[:ja:調伊企儺|調伊企儺]] * [[:ja:大葉子|大葉子]] {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]][[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

{{基礎情報 公家|氏名=倭手彦|画像=|画像サイズ=|画像説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=}}'''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and [[:ja:生没年不詳|death unknown]] ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Wa kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was [[直 (姓)|Atai]] == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=ISSE|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986|jstor=26864868}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web|title=海部氏系図|trans-title=Amebe shikeizu|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777|publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]|language=Japanese|accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[ja:倭手彦]][[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). When Silla made a fake surrender, Otokomaro did not accept the invitation and stopped his advance, but Kyokan fought even harder and, not understanding the meaning of the white flag raised by the Silla side, also raised the white flag and advanced. As a result, the Silla side attacked again, causing heavy losses to the vanguard forces. Wa no Kuni no Miyatsuko Tehiko, who had been fighting under Nenkan, decided that it was no longer possible to turn the tide of battle and withdrew from the front lines. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * [[:ja:飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[:ja:調伊企儺|調伊企儺]] * [[:ja:大葉子|大葉子]] {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]]{{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders]] [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

His Kabane was [[直 (姓)|Atai]] == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} '''Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' was a title held by the clan{{Efn|Yamato no Atai clan}} who ruled the central region of the later [[Yamato Province]].{{Cite journal|last=Yōko|first=ISSE|date=2019|title=Revisiting Tsuda Sōkichi in Postwar Japan: "Misunderstandings" and the Historical Facts of the Kiki|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/26864868|journal=Japan Review|issue=34|pages=139–160|issn=0915-0986|jstor=26864868}} Kuni no Miyatsuko were regional rulers subordinate to the [[Emperor of Japan]]. After the position was abolished they remained prominent as the priests of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]. Other kuni no miyatsuko this happened to include the [[Izumo clan]] of [[Izumo-taisha]], the [[Aso clan]] of [[Aso Shrine]], the [[Owari clan]] of [[Atsuta Shrine]], the {{ill|Munakata clan|ja|宗像氏}} of [[Munakata Taisha]],https://archive.today/20231025020641/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8839 and the [[Amabe clan]] of [[Kono Shrine]]{{cite web|title=海部氏系図|trans-title=Amebe shikeizu|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/201/777|publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]]|language=Japanese|accessdate=August 20, 2020}} [[ja:倭手彦]][[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} == record == In 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei's reign]] ), after the Mimana government was destroyed by Silla 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei fervently wished to restore [[:ja:任那日本府|the Mimana Nihonfu]] . According to Volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, [[681|in 681]] (April 10th year of [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu's reign]] ), a member of the clan, Yamato Naotatsumaro, was given the surname " [[Muraji]] " 『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条, and in September 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, the surname "Muraji" was bestowed on all members of the Yamato clan. 『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条 With the establishment of the eight-rank system of surnames, in 685 (June 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Yamato]] clan, a clan descended from [[Clan|the kuni]] no miyatsuko, was given the surname Ikimi . 『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 Volume 19 of the " [[Nihon Shoki]] " contains the story of [[:ja:紀男麻呂|Kino Omaro]], the commander-in-chief of the army that conquered [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. When Silla made a fake surrender, Otokomaro did not accept the invitation and stopped his advance, but Kyokan fought even harder and, not understanding the meaning of the white flag raised by the Silla side, also raised the white flag and advanced. As a result, the Silla side attacked again, causing heavy losses to the vanguard forces. Wa no Kuni no Miyatsuko Tehiko, who had been fighting under Nenkan, decided that it was no longer possible to turn the tide of battle and withdrew from the front lines. The Silla warrior took up a spear and pursued him, cornering him in a ditch in the castle and throwing his spear at him, but Tehiko rode his swift horse over the ditch and barely escaped. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * [[:ja:飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[:ja:調伊企儺|調伊企儺]] * [[:ja:大葉子|大葉子]] {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]]{{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders|en}} [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}
