[https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} == record == In 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei's reign]] ), after the Mimana government was destroyed by Silla 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei fervently wished to restore [[:ja:任那日本府|the Mimana Nihonfu]] . According to Volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, [[681|in 681]] (April 10th year of [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu's reign]] ), a member of the clan, Yamato Naotatsumaro, was given the surname " [[Muraji]] " 『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条, and in September 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, the surname "Muraji" was bestowed on all members of the Yamato clan. 『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条 With the establishment of the eight-rank system of surnames, in 685 (June 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Yamato]] clan, a clan descended from [[Clan|the kuni]] no miyatsuko, was given the surname Ikimi . 『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 Volume 19 of the " [[Nihon Shoki]] " contains the story of [[:ja:紀男麻呂|Kino Omaro]], the commander-in-chief of the army that conquered [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. When Silla made a fake surrender, Otokomaro did not accept the invitation and stopped his advance, but Kyokan fought even harder and, not understanding the meaning of the white flag raised by the Silla side, also raised the white flag and advanced. As a result, the Silla side attacked again, causing heavy losses to the vanguard forces. Wa no Kuni no Miyatsuko Tehiko, who had been fighting under Nenkan, decided that it was no longer possible to turn the tide of battle and withdrew from the front lines. The Silla warrior took up a spear and pursued him, cornering him in a ditch in the castle and throwing his spear at him, but Tehiko rode his swift horse over the ditch and barely escaped. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * [[:ja:飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[:ja:調伊企儺|調伊企儺]] * [[:ja:大葉子|大葉子]] {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]]{{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders|en}} [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

{{基礎情報 公家|氏名=倭手彦|画像=|画像サイズ=|画像説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=}}'''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and [[:ja:生没年不詳|death unknown]] ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Wa kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was [[直 (姓)|Atai]] == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:倭手彦]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * [[:ja:飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[:ja:調伊企儺|調伊企儺]] * [[:ja:大葉子|大葉子]] {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]]{{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders|en}} [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} == record == In 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei's reign]] ), after the Mimana government was destroyed by Silla 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei fervently wished to restore [[:ja:任那日本府|the Mimana Nihonfu]] . According to Volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, [[681|in 681]] (April 10th year of [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu's reign]] ), a member of the clan, Yamato Naotatsumaro, was given the surname " [[Muraji]] " 『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条, and in September 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, the surname "Muraji" was bestowed on all members of the Yamato clan. 『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条 With the establishment of the eight-rank system of surnames, in 685 (June 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Yamato]] clan, a clan descended from [[Clan|the kuni]] no miyatsuko, was given the surname Ikimi . 『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 Volume 19 of the " [[Nihon Shoki]] " contains the story of [[:ja:紀男麻呂|Kino Omaro]], the commander-in-chief of the army that conquered [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. When Silla made a fake surrender, Otokomaro did not accept the invitation and stopped his advance, but Kyokan fought even harder and, not understanding the meaning of the white flag raised by the Silla side, also raised the white flag and advanced. As a result, the Silla side attacked again, causing heavy losses to the vanguard forces. Wa no Kuni no Miyatsuko Tehiko, who had been fighting under Nenkan, decided that it was no longer possible to turn the tide of battle and withdrew from the front lines. The Silla warrior took up a spear and pursued him, cornering him in a ditch in the castle and throwing his spear at him, but Tehiko rode his swift horse over the ditch and barely escaped. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * {{ill|10=ja|11=飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=調伊企儺|調伊企儺}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=大葉子|大葉子}} {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]]{{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders|en}} [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

{{基礎情報 公家|氏名=倭手彦|画像=|画像サイズ=|画像説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=}}'''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and [[:ja:生没年不詳|death unknown]] ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Wa kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was [[直 (姓)|Atai]] == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:倭手彦]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * [[Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso]] == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:倭手彦]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} == record == In 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei's reign]] ), after the Mimana government was destroyed by Silla 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei fervently wished to restore [[:ja:任那日本府|the Mimana Nihonfu]] . According to Volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, [[681|in 681]] (April 10th year of [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu's reign]] ), a member of the clan, Yamato Naotatsumaro, was given the surname " [[Muraji]] " 『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条, and in September 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, the surname "Muraji" was bestowed on all members of the Yamato clan. 『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条 With the establishment of the eight-rank system of surnames, in 685 (June 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Yamato]] clan, a clan descended from [[Clan|the kuni]] no miyatsuko, was given the surname Ikimi . 『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 Volume 19 of the " [[Nihon Shoki]] " contains the story of [[:ja:紀男麻呂|Kino Omaro]], the commander-in-chief of the army that conquered [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. When Silla made a fake surrender, Otokomaro did not accept the invitation and stopped his advance, but Kyokan fought even harder and, not understanding the meaning of the white flag raised by the Silla side, also raised the white flag and advanced. As a result, the Silla side attacked again, causing heavy losses to the vanguard forces. Wa no Kuni no Miyatsuko Tehiko, who had been fighting under Nenkan, decided that it was no longer possible to turn the tide of battle and withdrew from the front lines. The Silla warrior took up a spear and pursued him, cornering him in a ditch in the castle and throwing his spear at him, but Tehiko rode his swift horse over the ditch and barely escaped. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Related item == * {{ill|10=ja|11=飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|List of people before the Asuka period}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=調伊企儺|Tsuki no Ikina}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=大葉子|Obako}} {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]]{{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders|en}} [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

{{基礎情報 公家|氏名=倭手彦|画像=|画像サイズ=|画像説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=}}'''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and [[:ja:生没年不詳|death unknown]] ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Wa kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was [[直 (姓)|Atai]] == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},

This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|List of people before the Asuka period}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=調伊企儺|Tsuki no Ikina}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=大葉子|Obako}} {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]]{{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders|en}} [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

{{基礎情報 公家|氏名=倭手彦|画像=|画像サイズ=|画像説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=}}'''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and [[:ja:生没年不詳|death unknown]] ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Wa kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was [[直 (姓)|Atai]] == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}}{{Infobox Japanese clan|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|founder={{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[ja:倭手彦]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] == History == [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|[[Hibara Shrine]], at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During [[Jimmu's Eastern Expedition]] {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} There is a complex myth about the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]] and its link to the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] and [[Amaterasu]]. There was a crisis during his reign and eventually the worship of [[Amaterasu]] and [[Yamato Okunitama]] were moved out of the imperial palace to separate shrines. Worship of Amaterasu moved to [[Hibara Shrine]] and then many other shrines called {{ill|Moto-Ise Shrines|lt=|ja|元伊勢}} until eventually reaching [[Ise Jingu]]. By contrast the worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] moved to [[Oyamato Shrine]], near [[Hibara Shrine]] and [[Omiwa Shrine]]. Yamato Okunitama was first entrusted to a daughter of [[Emperor Sujin]] named {{Ill|Nunaki-iri-hime|ja|渟名城入姫命}}, but shortly afterwards, her health began to fail. It is recorded that she became emaciated and lost all of her hair, which rendered her unable to perform her duties.{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 2|author=Aston, William George.|date=1896|publisher=The Japan Society London|isbn=9780524053478|pages=150–164|author-link=William George Aston}} These efforts still did not alleviate the ongoing plague, so Sujin decreed that a [[divination]] be performed sometime during the 7th year of his reign, that would involve him making a trip to the plain of Kami-asaji, and invoking the [[Yaoyorozu no Kami|eighty myriad deities]]. After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|lt=Agoko no Sukune|ja|倭吾子籠}} was a notable member of the clan and governor of Yamato Province{{Cite web|date=April 2022|title=Episode 61: Bad Behavior and the People that Excuse It|url=https://sengokudaimyo.com/podcast/episode-61|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Sengoku Daimyo|language=en-US}} == record == In 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei's reign]] ), after the Mimana government was destroyed by Silla 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei fervently wished to restore [[:ja:任那日本府|the Mimana Nihonfu]] . According to Volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, [[681|in 681]] (April 10th year of [[Emperor Tenmu|Emperor Tenmu's reign]] ), a member of the clan, Yamato Naotatsumaro, was given the surname " [[Muraji]] " 『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条, and in September 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, the surname "Muraji" was bestowed on all members of the Yamato clan. 『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条 With the establishment of the eight-rank system of surnames, in 685 (June 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the [[Kuni no miyatsuko|Yamato]] clan, a clan descended from [[Clan|the kuni]] no miyatsuko, was given the surname Ikimi . 『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 Volume 19 of the " [[Nihon Shoki]] " contains the story of [[:ja:紀男麻呂|Kino Omaro]], the commander-in-chief of the army that conquered [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. When Silla made a fake surrender, Otokomaro did not accept the invitation and stopped his advance, but Kyokan fought even harder and, not understanding the meaning of the white flag raised by the Silla side, also raised the white flag and advanced. As a result, the Silla side attacked again, causing heavy losses to the vanguard forces. Wa no Kuni no Miyatsuko Tehiko, who had been fighting under Nenkan, decided that it was no longer possible to turn the tide of battle and withdrew from the front lines. The Silla warrior took up a spear and pursued him, cornering him in a ditch in the castle and throwing his spear at him, but Tehiko rode his swift horse over the ditch and barely escaped. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|List of people before the Asuka period}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=調伊企儺|Tsuki no Ikina}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=大葉子|Obako}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. The fighting general was so frustrated that he couldn't help but shout out loud. It is unclear what became of Yamatohiko after that, as there is no record of it. 『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See also == * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|List of people before the Asuka period}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=調伊企儺|Tsuki no Ikina}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=大葉子|Obako}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no Miyatsuko]]{{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders|en}} [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}}

revision.text:
{{infobox royalty|氏名=倭手彦|image=|imageサイズ=|image説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|caption=[[Ōyamato Shrine]], the main shrine of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}|father='''{{ill|Yukitei no Choku|ja|由岐庭乃直}}'''|issue='''{{ill|Echi|ja|邑智}}'''{{efn|Provincial Lord during {{ill|Empress Suiko|en}}'s reign, Likely a descendant rather than a son}}}}{{draft}}{{User:Immanuelle/Promising Draft}}{{complex}} '''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and death unknown ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Yamato kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was {{ill|10=ja|11=直 (姓)|Atai}} He served under [[Emperor Kinmei]] and participated in the Silla war == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[Category:dd]][[ja:倭手彦]] [[Category:dd]][[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. The subsequent fate of Tehiko remains unrecorded and, thus, unknown.『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 According to volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, in the year 681 (April of the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), a clan member by the name of Yamato Naotatsumaro was granted the surname "{{ill|Muraji|en}}".『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条 By September of the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, this surname was conferred upon all members of the Yamato clan. Following the establishment of the eight-rank surname system in 685 (June of the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the Yamato clan, descendants of the kuni no miyatsuko, were bestowed the surname Ikimi.『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See also == * '''{{ill|Yamato no Tehiko|ja|倭手彦|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|lt1=Yamato no Tehiko|lt2=手彦|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Yamato no Iotari|ja|大倭五百足|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|lt1=Yamato no Iotari|lt2=五百足|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|ja|倭吾子籠|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|lt1=Yamogokoro no Sukune|lt2=倭吾子籠宿祢|complete=true|en:draft}} * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|List of people before the Asuka period}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=調伊企儺|Tsuki no Ikina}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=大葉子|Obako}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yamato no Tehiko]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] ]] {{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders|en}} [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}} {{User:Immanuelle/Furutama Faith}}

the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[Category:dd]][[ja:倭手彦]] [[Category:dd]][[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. The subsequent fate of Tehiko remains unrecorded and, thus, unknown.『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条 According to volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, in the year 681 (April of the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), a clan member by the name of Yamato Naotatsumaro was granted the surname "{{ill|Muraji|en}}".『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条 By September of the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, this surname was conferred upon all members of the Yamato clan. Following the establishment of the eight-rank surname system in 685 (June of the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the Yamato clan, descendants of the kuni no miyatsuko, were bestowed the surname Ikimi.『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See also == * '''{{ill|Yamato no Tehiko|ja|倭手彦|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|lt1=Yamato no Tehiko|lt2=手彦|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Yamato no Iotari|ja|大倭五百足|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|lt1=Yamato no Iotari|lt2=五百足|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|ja|倭吾子籠|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|lt1=Yamogokoro no Sukune|lt2=倭吾子籠宿祢|complete=true|en:draft}} * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|List of people before the Asuka period}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=調伊企儺|Tsuki no Ikina}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=大葉子|Obako}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} {{draft categories|[[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORTYamato no Tehiko}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORTYamato no Tehiko}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORTYamato no Tehiko}}[[category:fix the O]] [[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:dd]][[en:Yamato no Tehiko]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]][[Category:Pages from Simple English Wikipedia]] [[Category:bump-01-18]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:fix the O]]{{DEFAULTSORTYamato no Tehiko}}[[category:fix the O]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:bump1-19]] [[Category:refreshing categories imported on 2024-01-19]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[simple:user:immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]][[simple:user:immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] ]] {{ill|Category:Kuni no miyatsuko position holders|en}} [[simple:Immanuelle/{{PAGENAME}}]][[fr:{{PAGENAME}}]]}} {{User:Immanuelle/Furutama Faith}}
