1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[ja:倭手彦]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. The subsequent fate of Tehiko remains unrecorded and, thus, unknown.『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条 According to volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, in the year 681 (April of the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), a clan member by the name of Yamato Naotatsumaro was granted the surname "{{ill|Muraji|en}}".『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu10th year 4月12日条 By September of the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, this surname was conferred upon all members of the Yamato clan. Following the establishment of the eight-rank surname system in 685 (June of the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the Yamato clan, descendants of the kuni no miyatsuko, were bestowed the surname Ikimi.『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu14th year 6月20日条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} Tehiko|lt2=手彦|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Yamato no Iotari|ja|大倭五百足|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|lt1=Yamato no Iotari|lt2=五百足|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|ja|倭吾子籠|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|lt1=Yamogokoro no Sukune|lt2=倭吾子籠宿祢|complete=true|en:draft}} * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} * 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{infobox royalty|氏名=倭手彦|image=|imageサイズ=|image説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|caption=[[Ōyamato Shrine]], the main shrine of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}|father='''{{ill|Yukitei no Choku|ja|由岐庭乃直}}'''|issue='''{{ill|Echi|ja|邑智}}'''{{efn|Provincial Lord during {{ill|Empress Suiko|en}}'s reign, Likely a descendant rather than a son}}}} {{complex}} '''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and death unknown ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Yamato kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was {{ill|10=ja|11=直 (姓)|Atai}} He served under [[Emperor Kinmei]] and participated in the Silla war == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[ja:倭手彦]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. {{draft}} {{Promising draft|reason=the high amount of work put into it over a long time, see edit history}} {{infobox royalty|氏名=倭手彦|image=|imageサイズ=|image説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|caption=[[Ōyamato Shrine]], the main shrine of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|province of origin=[[Yamato Province]]|home province=[[Yamato Province]]|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}|father='''{{ill|Yukitei no Choku|ja|由岐庭乃直}}'''|issue='''{{ill|Echi|ja|邑智}}'''{{efn|Provincial Lord during {{ill|Empress Suiko|en}}'s reign, Likely a descendant rather than a son}}}} {{complex}} '''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and death unknown ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Yamato kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was {{ill|10=ja|11=直 (姓)|Atai}} He served under [[Emperor Kinmei]] and participated in the Silla war == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[ja:倭手彦]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. The subsequent fate of Tehiko remains unrecorded and, thus, unknown.『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条 According to volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, in the year 681 (April of the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), a clan member by the name of Yamato Naotatsumaro was granted the surname "{{ill|Muraji|en}}".『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu10th year 4月12日条 By September of the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, this surname was conferred upon all members of the Yamato clan. Following the establishment of the eight-rank surname system in 685 (June of the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the Yamato clan, descendants of the kuni no miyatsuko, were bestowed the surname Ikimi.『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu14th year 6月20日条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See also == * '''{{ill|Yamato no Tehiko|ja|倭手彦|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|lt1=Yamato no Tehiko|lt2=手彦|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Yamato no Iotari|ja|大倭五百足|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|lt1=Yamato no Iotari|lt2=五百足|complete=true|en:draft}}''' * {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|ja|倭吾子籠|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|lt1=Yamogokoro no Sukune|lt2=倭吾子籠宿祢|complete=true|en:draft}} * {{ill|Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en||fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|List of people before the Asuka period}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=調伊企儺|Tsuki no Ikina}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=大葉子|Obako}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Notes == {{notelist}} === References === {{reflist}} === Bibliography === {{Refbegin}} ====''Nihon Shoki''==== ''See the references under {{Lang|ja-latn|[[Nihon Shoki]]}} for an extended bibliography'' *{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. (1979). [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[Louis-Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} [[ja:倭手彦]] [[en:draft:Yamato no Tehiko]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. The subsequent fate of Tehiko remains unrecorded and, thus, unknown.『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条 According to volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, in the year 681 (April of the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), a clan member by the name of Yamato Naotatsumaro was granted the surname "{{ill|Muraji|en}}".『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu10th year 4月12日条 By September of the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, this surname was conferred upon all members of the Yamato clan. Following the establishment of the eight-rank surname system in 685 (June of the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the Yamato clan, descendants of the kuni no miyatsuko, were bestowed the surname Ikimi.『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu14th year 6月20日条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the {{ill|Owari clan|ja|尾張氏|en}} and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市}}|father='''{{ill|Yukitei no Choku|ja|由岐庭乃直}}'''|issue='''{{ill|Echi|ja|邑智}}'''{{efn|Provincial Lord during {{ill|Empress Suiko|en}}'s reign, Likely a descendant rather than a son}}}} {{complex}} '''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and death unknown ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Yamato kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was {{ill|10=ja|11=直 (姓)|Atai}} He served under [[Emperor Kinmei]] and participated in the Silla war == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[Mount Miwa]] in [[Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦}} was given the position of governor of [[Yamato Province]] by [[Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} revision.text:
{{基礎情報 公家 | 氏名 = 倭手彦 | 画像 = | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = | 時代 = [[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]] | 生誕 = 不明 | 死没 = 不明 | 改名 = | 諡号 = | 神号 = | 戒名 = | 墓所 = | 官位 = [[倭国造]] | 主君 = [[欽明天皇]] | 氏族 = [[倭氏|倭直]] | 父母 = | 兄弟 = | 妻 = | 子 = }} '''倭 手彦'''（やまと の てひこ、[[生没年不詳]]）は、[[古墳時代]]の[[豪族]]で'''[[倭国造]]'''の一人。[[カバネ|姓]]は[[直 (姓)|直]]。
== 出自 == 倭氏（[[倭国造]]家）は[[大和国]][[城下郡]]大和郷（おおやまとごう）の豪族で、この地域は現在の[[奈良県]][[天理市]]の南部、佐保町大和にあたる。『[[新撰姓氏録]]』「大和国神別」では「出自神知津彦命也」と記されており、『[[日本書紀]]』巻第三では[[椎根津彦]]（しいねつひこ）<ref>『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条</ref>、『[[古事記]]』では槁根津日子（さおねつひこ）を祖とする<ref>『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条</ref>、となっている。
== 記録 == 西暦[[562年]]（[[欽明天皇]]23年）、任那の官家が新羅に滅ぼされてから<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年1月条</ref>、欽明天皇は[[任那日本府]]の再興を悲願とするようになった。
『[[日本書紀]]』巻第十九には、[[新羅]]征討軍の大将軍、[[紀男麻呂]]（き の おまろ）と[[河辺瓊缶]]（かわべ の にえ）の物語がある。男麻呂は新羅が偽装降伏してきた時点で誘いには乗らずに進軍を止めたが、瓊缶はさらに奮戦し、新羅側が白旗をあげた意味を理解せずに、同じように白旗をあげて進んでいった。そのため、新羅側の再攻撃を受け、先鋒の軍に大損害を与えた。
瓊缶の部下として参戦していた倭国造手彦は、もはや戦況を挽回することは不可能だと判断し、戦線から離脱した。新羅の闘将（いくさのきみ）は手に矛をとって追いかけ、彼を城の溝に追い詰め、矛を投げたが、手彦は駿馬を操り、溝を乗り越えて辛うじて身を脱した。闘将はくやしがって、思わず声に出して叫んでいた。
この戦いで、新羅の闘将は瓊缶や同行していた婦女たちを捕虜にした。瓊缶は妻の[[甘美媛]]（うましひめ）を身代わりにして、解放され、生きながらえた。妻は闘将の妾にされ、その後解放されたが、瓊缶の言うことに従おうとはしなかった。
倭手彦がその後どうなったのかは、記録されていないため、不明である<ref>『日本書紀』欽明天皇23年7月条</ref>。
『書紀』巻第二十九によると、[[681年]]（[[天武天皇]]10年4月）、一族の倭直竜麻呂は「[[連]]」の[[カバネ|姓]]を与えられ<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇10年4月12日条</ref>、天武天皇12年9月には、倭氏一族すべてに「連」姓が授けられた<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇12年9月2日条</ref>。そして、[[八色の姓]]制定により、[[685年]]（天武天皇14年6月）、[[国造]]系[[氏族]]である大倭氏（やまとうじ）は[[忌寸]]を賜姓されている<ref>『日本書紀』天武天皇14年6月20日条</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『日本書紀』（一）・(三)・（五）（[[岩波文庫]]、1994年、1995年） *[[宇治谷孟]]訳 『日本書紀』全現代語訳（上）・(下）（[[講談社学術文庫]]、1988年） *[[井上光貞]]『日本の歴史1 神話から歴史へ』（[[中央公論社]]、1965年） *[[佐伯有清]]編『日本古代氏族事典【新装版】』（[[雄山閣]]、2015年）
== 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] *[[調伊企儺]] *[[大葉子]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:やまと の てひこ}} [[Category:古墳時代の人物]] [[Category:古代日本の人物]] [[Category:倭氏|てひこ]] [[Category:国造]] [[Category:古代日朝関係]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

revision.text:
[[fr:Yamato no Tehiko]][[ja:倭手彦]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|father={{ill|Yukitei no Atai|qid=Q134863513}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55407038]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Tehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox royalty|氏名=倭手彦|image=|imageサイズ=|image説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|caption=[[Ōyamato Shrine]], the main shrine of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}|father='''{{ill|Yukitei no Choku|ja|由岐庭乃直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukitei no Choku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''|issue='''{{ill|Echi|ja|邑智|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echi|qq=}}'''{{efn|Provincial Lord during {{ill|Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko|2=ja|3=推古天皇|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko|qq=}}'s reign, Likely a descendant rather than a son}}}} '''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and death unknown ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōzoku|qq=}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Yamato kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was {{ill|10=ja|11=直 (姓)|Atai|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} He served under [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and participated in the Silla war == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|qq=}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[:en:Silla|Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. The subsequent fate of Tehiko remains unrecorded and, thus, unknown.『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条 According to volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, in the year 681 (April of the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), a clan member by the name of Yamato Naotatsumaro was granted the surname "{{ill|Muraji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraji|qq=}}".『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu10th year 4月12日条 By September of the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, this surname was conferred upon all members of the Yamato clan. Following the establishment of the eight-Rank surname system in 685 (June of the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the Yamato clan, descendants of the kuni no miyatsuko, were bestowed the surname Ikimi.『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu14th year 6月20日条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the [[:en:Owari clan|Owari clan]] and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} [[fr:Yamato no Tehiko]][[ja:倭手彦]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|father={{ill|Yukitei no Atai|WD=Q134863513}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55407038]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Tehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox royalty|氏名=倭手彦|image=|imageサイズ=|image説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|caption=[[Ōyamato Shrine]], the main shrine of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}|father='''{{ill|Yukitei no Choku|ja|由岐庭乃直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukitei no Choku|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''|issue='''{{ill|Echi|ja|邑智|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echi|qq=}}'''{{efn|Provincial Lord during {{ill|Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko|2=ja|3=推古天皇|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko|qq=}}'s reign, Likely a descendant rather than a son}}}} '''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and death unknown ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōzoku|qq=}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Yamato kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was {{ill|10=ja|11=直 (姓)|Atai|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} He served under [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and participated in the Silla war == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|qq=}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by [[fr:Yamato no Tehiko]][[ja:倭手彦]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|father={{ill|Yukitei no Atai|WD=Q134863513}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55407038]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Tehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox royalty|氏名=倭手彦|image=|imageサイズ=|image説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|caption=[[Ōyamato Shrine]], the main shrine of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}|father='''{{ill|Yukitei no Choku|ja|由岐庭乃直|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukitei no Choku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''|issue='''{{ill|Echi|ja|邑智|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echi|qq=}}'''{{efn|Provincial Lord during {{ill|Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko|2=ja|3=推古天皇|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko|qq=}}'s reign, Likely a descendant rather than a son}}}} '''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and death unknown ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōzoku|qq=}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Yamato kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was {{ill|10=ja|11=直 (姓)|Atai|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} He served under [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and participated in the Silla war == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|qq=}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[:en:Silla|Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. The subsequent fate of Tehiko remains unrecorded and, thus, unknown.『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条 According to volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, in the year 681 (April of the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), a clan member by the name of Yamato Naotatsumaro was granted the surname "{{ill|Muraji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraji|qq=}}".『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu10th year 4月12日条 By September of the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, this surname was conferred upon all members of the Yamato clan. Following the establishment of the eight-Rank surname system in 685 (June of the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the Yamato clan, descendants of the kuni no miyatsuko, were bestowed the surname Ikimi.『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu14th year 6月20日条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the [[:en:Owari clan|Owari clan]] and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|qq=}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[:en:Silla|Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. The subsequent fate of Tehiko remains unrecorded and, thus, unknown.『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条 According to volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, in the year 681 (April of the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), a clan member by the name of Yamato Naotatsumaro was granted the surname "{{ill|Muraji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraji|qq=}}".『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu10th year 4月12日条 By September of the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, this surname was conferred upon all members of the Yamato clan. Following the establishment of the eight-Rank surname system in 685 (June of the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the Yamato clan, descendants of the kuni no miyatsuko, were bestowed the surname Ikimi.『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu14th year 6月20日条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the [[:en:Owari clan|Owari clan]] and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} [[fr:Yamato no Tehiko]][[ja:倭手彦]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|father={{ill|Yukitei no Atai|WD=Q134863513}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55407038]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Tehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox royalty|氏名=倭手彦|image=|imageサイズ=|image説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|caption=[[Ōyamato Shrine]], the main shrine of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}|father='''{{ill|Yukitei no Choku|ja|由岐庭乃直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukitei no Choku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''|issue='''{{ill|Echi|ja|邑智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echi|qq=}}'''{{efn|Provincial Lord during {{ill|Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko|2=ja|3=推古天皇|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko|qq=}}'s reign, Likely a descendant rather than a son}}}} '''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and death unknown ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōzoku|qq=}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Yamato kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was {{ill|10=ja|11=直 (姓)|Atai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} He served under [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and participated in the Silla war == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family|qq=draft}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. revision.text:
[[fr:Yamato no Tehiko]][[ja:倭手彦]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|father={{ill|Yukitei no Atai|WD=Q134863513}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q55407038]] Wikidata name: Yamato no Tehiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:26, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox royalty|氏名=倭手彦|image=|imageサイズ=|image説明=|時代=[[上古#日本|上古]]・[[古墳時代]]|生誕=不明|死没=不明|改名=|諡号=|神号=|戒名=|墓所=|官位=[[倭国造]]|主君=[[欽明天皇]]|氏族=[[倭氏|倭直]]|父母=|兄弟=|妻=|子=|image=Ohyamato-jinja Haiden.jpg|image caption=Oyamato Shrine, their main shrine|caption=[[Ōyamato Shrine]], the main shrine of the [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]|province of origin=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|home province=[[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]]|founder={{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shinetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}},
{{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}|father='''{{ill|Yukitei no Choku|ja|由岐庭乃直|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yukitei no Choku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}'''|issue='''{{ill|Echi|ja|邑智|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Echi|qq=}}'''{{efn|Provincial Lord during {{ill|Empress Suiko|1=Empress Suiko|2=ja|3=推古天皇|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Suiko|qq=}}'s reign, Likely a descendant rather than a son}}}} '''Yamato no Tehiko''' (year of birth and death unknown ) was a {{ill|Gōzoku|lt=powerful|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōzoku|qq=}} clan leader in [[Kofun period|the Kofun period]] and one of '''[[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|the Yamato kuni no miyatsuko]]''' . His Kabane was {{ill|10=ja|11=直 (姓)|Atai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} He served under [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]] and participated in the Silla war == Origin == The Wa clan (Wa koku no [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko|miyatsuko family]] ) was a powerful clan in [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato-go]], Joka - gun, Yamato Province, which currently corresponds to Yamato, Saho-cho, in the southern part of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri City]], [[:en:Nara Prefecture|Nara Prefecture]] . The {{ill|Shinsen Shōjiroku|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinsen Shōjiroku|qq=}} 's "Yamato no Kuni Kamiwake" section states that "his descendants were Kamichitsuhiko no Mikoto," while [[Nihon Shoki|the Nihon Shoki]], Volume 3 states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|lt=Shiinetsuhiko|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『日本書紀』神武天皇即位前紀甲寅年10月5日条, and the [[Kojiki]] states that his ancestor was {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|fr|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} 『古事記』中巻、神武天皇条 . {{Short description|Japanese noble family|qq=draft}} Their [[Ujigami]] or clan god is [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] of [[Ōyamato Shrine]]{{Cite web|title=International Symposium "Perspectives on Japanese history and literature from ancient historical records"|url=https://www.waseda.jp/inst/sgu/news-en/2019/04/12/5171|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Top Global University Project: Waseda Goes Global|language=en}} Some scholars interpret the ''kami'' as being a variant or epithet of [[Ōmononushi]] who has much more widespread worship.{{Cite journal|last=Ellwood|first=Robert S.|date=1990|title=The Sujin Religious Revolution|journal=Japanese Journal of Religious Studies|volume=17|issue=2/3|pages=199–217|doi=10.18874/jjrs.17.2-3.1990.199-217|issn=0304-1042|jstor=30234018|doi-access=free}}{{Cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=_Q81DQAAQBAJ|title=Shinto: A History|last=Hardacre|first=Helen|date=2017|publisher=Oxford University Press|isbn=978-0-19-062171-1|language=en}}{{rp|22}} There is a complex myth about the origins of modern worship of [[:en:Yamato Okunitama|Yamato Okunitama]] during the reign of [[:en:Emperor Sujin|Emperor Sujin]].{{Cite web|last=D|first=John|date=2012-11-10|title=Teeuwen on Shinto|url=https://www.greenshinto.com/2012/11/10/teeuwen-on-shinto/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Green Shinto|language=en-GB}}https://www.japanpolicyforum.jp/pdf/2016/no35/DJweb_35_cul_02.pdfhttps://freidok.uni-freiburg.de/fedora/objects/freidok:4635/datastreams/FILE1/content{{Citation|title=Book V|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_V|work=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697, Volume 1|access-date=2023-05-04}} [[File:Hibara-jinja,_torii_closeup.jpg|thumb|240x240px|Hibara Shrine, at the foot of [[:en:Mount Miwa|Mount Miwa]] in [[:en:Sakurai, Nara|Sakurai, Nara]], identified as the place where the Yata-no-Kagami and the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi were first enshrined after their removal from the imperial palace.]] During {{ill|Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jimmu%27s Eastern Expedition|qq=}} {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} was given the position of governor of [[:en:Yamato Province|Yamato Province]] by [[:en:Emperor Jimmu|Emperor Jimmu]].{{Cite web|title=Friday: Kojiki (「乞食」ではなく『古事記』ですヨ！！) #26|url=https://japaneseexperts.net/972/|access-date=2023-05-04|website=Japanese Experts Net|language=en-US}} And Saonetsuhiko became their ancestor.{{Cite web|date=2023-10-26|title=Saonetsuhiko {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム|url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231026183115/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9159|archive-date=2023-10-26|access-date=2023-12-04}} After the divination, {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}}, a descendant of {{ill|Saonetsuhiko|ja|椎根津彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saonetsuhiko|qq=}} would conduct the rites pertaining to Okunitama, replacing the emaciated Nunaki-iri-hime. {{ill|Ichishi no Nagaochi|ja|市磯長尾市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichishi no Nagaochi|qq=}} would be the ancestor of the Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko.{{Cite web|title=Page:Nihongi by Aston.djvu/208|url=https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Page:Nihongi_by_Aston.djvu/208|access-date=2023-10-24|website=en.wikisource.org|language=en}} == Biography == In the year 562 AD (the 23rd year of [[:en:Emperor Kinmei|Emperor Kinmei]]'s reign), following the destruction of the Mimana region by Silla『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 1月条, Emperor Kinmei earnestly desired the reestablishment of the {{ill|10=ja|11=任那日本府|lt2=任那日本府|Mimana Nihonfu|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}. The Nihon Shoki, in its 19th volume, narrates the tale of {{ill|10=ja|11=紀男麻呂|lt2=紀男麻呂|Ki no Omaro|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}}, the commanding general of the expeditionary force against [[:en:Silla|Silla]], and Kawabe no Nie. Ki no Omaro, upon facing a feigned surrender from Silla, wisely halted his advance rather than falling for the deceit. However, Kawabe no Nie, not grasping the significance behind Silla's raising of the white flag, mistakenly did the same and advanced, leading to a renewed attack from Silla that inflicted heavy casualties on the vanguard. Tehiko, a provincial governor (kuni no miyatsuko) of Wa, serving under Kawabe no Nie, realized that reversing the course of the battle was no longer feasible and retreated from the frontline. A Silla commander, wielding a spear, chased him down, cornering him at a moat within the fortress. Although the commander hurled his spear at Tehiko, Tehiko managed to narrowly escape by riding his swift horse across the moat. The Silla commander, in frustration, couldn't help but cry out loud. The battle concluded with the Silla commander taking Kawabe no Nie and accompanying women as prisoners. Kawabe no Nie was eventually released in exchange for his wife, Umanishihime, who had been made a concubine by the commander. Even after being released, she refused to obey Kawabe no Nie. The subsequent fate of Tehiko remains unrecorded and, thus, unknown.『日本書紀』欽明天皇23rd year 7月条 According to volume 29 of the Nihon Shoki, in the year 681 (April of the 10th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), a clan member by the name of Yamato Naotatsumaro was granted the surname "{{ill|Muraji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muraji|qq=}}".『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu10th year 4月12日条 By September of the 12th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign, this surname was conferred upon all members of the Yamato clan. Following the establishment of the eight-Rank surname system in 685 (June of the 14th year of Emperor Tenmu's reign), the Yamato clan, descendants of the kuni no miyatsuko, were bestowed the surname Ikimi.『日本書紀』Emperor Tenmu14th year 6月20日条 == Family tree == Many clans may be descendants of Furutama no Mikoto. This includes the [[:en:Owari clan|Owari clan]] and [[Yamato no Kuni no Miyatsuko]]. This is a subject of debate.{{Cite web|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160422190938/http://wwr2.ucom.ne.jp/hetoyc15/keihu/amabe/amabe-k2.htm|access-date=2023-12-13|website=web.archive.org}} {{User:Immanuelle/Generations of Watatsumi}} == See also == * '''{{ill|Yamato no Tehiko|ja|倭手彦|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|lt1=Yamato no Tehiko|lt2=手彦|complete=true|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Yamato no Iotari|ja|大倭五百足|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|lt1=Yamato no Iotari|lt2=五百足|complete=true|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Iotari|qq=}}''' * {{ill|Yamato no Agoko|ja|倭吾子籠|simple|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|fr|User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|lt1=Yamogokoro no Sukune|lt2=倭吾子籠宿祢|complete=true|en:draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Agoko|qq=}} * {{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine (disambiguation)|lt=Yamato Okunitama Shrine|ja|大和大国魂神社|simple|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamato Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} * [[Kuni no miyatsuko]] * {{ill|Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|en|fr|Yamatototohimomoso-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Princess Yamato Totohi Momoso|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|List of people before the Asuka period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=調伊企儺|Tsuki no Ikina|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} * {{ill|10=ja|11=大葉子|Obako|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/10=ja|qq=}} == footnote == == References == * "Nihon Shoki" (vol. 1), (vol. 3), (vol. 5) ( Iwanami Bunko, 1994, 1995) * "Nihon Shoki" modern translation by Tsutomu Ujitani (vol. 1 and 2) ( Kodansha Academic Library, 1988) * Mitsusada Inoue, "Japanese History 1: From Myth to History" ( Chuokoron-Shinsha, 1965) * Saeki Ariyoshi (ed.), Dictionary of Ancient Japanese Clans (New Edition) ( Yuzankaku, 2015) == Notes == book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=1IJrNAKBpycC&pg=PA299|title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697|last=Aston|first=William George|publisher=Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner (for the Japan Society of London)|year=1896|isbn=9780524053478|volume=1|place=London|oclc=448337491|author-link=William George Aston}}, [https://books.google.com/books?id=_oEfAAAAYAAJ alt-link] English translation * {{cite web|author=JHTI|year=2002|title=Nihon Shoki|url=https://jhti.berkeley.edu/Nihon%20shoki.html|access-date=2019-08-23|work=[[Japanese Historical Text Initiative]] (JHTI)|publisher=UC Berkeley}}, searchtext resource to retrieve kanbun text vs. English tr. (Aston) in blocs. *{{cite book|title=Nihon shoki (日本書紀)|last=Ujiya|first=Tsutomu (宇治谷孟)|publisher=Kodansha|year=1988|isbn=978-0-8021-5058-5|volume=上}}, modern Japanese translation. *{{cite book|title=The Kojiki|last=Chamberlain|first=Basil Hall|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1919|oclc=1882339|author-link=Basil Hall Chamberlain}} [http://www.sacred-texts.com/shi/kj/index.htm sacred texts] *{{cite book|title=Shintei Kojiki (新訂 古事記)|last=Takeda|first=Yukichi (武田祐吉)|publisher=Kadokawa|year=1977|isbn=4-04-400101-4}}, annotated Japanese. ====Secondary sources==== * [[Delmer Brown|Brown, Delmer M.]] and Ichirō Ishida, eds. [https://books.google.com/books?id=w4f5FrmIJKIC&q=Gukansho ''Gukanshō: The Future and the Past''.] Berkeley: University of California Press. {{ISBN|978-0-520-03460-0}}; {{OCLC|251325323}} * [[:en:Louis Frédéric|Nussbaum, Louis-Frédéric]] and Käthe Roth. (2005). [https://books.google.com/books?id=p2QnPijAEmEC ''Japan encyclopedia''.] Cambridge: [[:en:Harvard University Press|Harvard University Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-674-01753-5}}; {{OCLC|58053128}} * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane, Richard Arthur Brabazon]]. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|194887}} * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[Nihon Ōdai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&q=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon''.] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. {{OCLC|5850691}} * [[H. Paul Varley|Varley, H. Paul.]] (1980). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tVv6OAAACAAJ ''Jinnō Shōtōki: A Chronicle of Gods and Sovereigns''.] New York: Columbia University Press. {{ISBN|978-0-231-04940-5}}; {{OCLC|59145842}} {{Refend}} == External links == * [https://web.archive.org/web/20100528074836/http://www.myj7000.jp-biz.net/clan/02/021/02140.htm 日本の苗字7000傑 姓氏類別大観 大倭国造] {{Miwa and Yamato Faith}} ]]}} }} {{translated page|ja|倭手彦|version=99874815|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] [[ja:倭手彦]] [[fr:User:Immanuelle/Yamato no Tehiko]] [[fr:Yamato no Tehiko]] [[ja:Yamato no Tehiko]] [[Category:Yamato clan]] [[Category:Japan–Korea Relations in Antiquity]] [[Category:People of ancient Japan]] [[Category:People of Kofun-period Japan]] [[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]][[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]] {{wikidata link|Q55407038}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]
